Explanation: Used to list all elements of a set explicitly.
Means that an element belongs to a set.
The element is not contained in the set.
Every element of A is also in B.
A ⊆ B and A ≠ B (B has extra elements).
At least one element of A is not in B.
B ⊆ A → A contains every element of B.
A ⊇ B and A ≠ B.
The set with no elements.
Number of elements in set A.
The set of all subsets of A.
All elements in A or B (or both).
Elements that A and B have in common.
Elements in A that are not in B.
All elements not in A (relative to universal set U).
All ordered pairs (x, y) with x∈A, y∈B.
Used in logical definitions involving sets.
Example: ∃x (x ∈ A) → at least one x in A.

Explanation: Universal quantifier in set definitions.
Used in set-builder notation.
A and B have exactly the same elements.
The sets differ by at least one element.
